q: COPY INTO vs Auto Loader: when do you use each for ingesting files from cloud storage into Delta?
a: The decision is volume-driven. COPY INTO is a SQL command that tracks which files have already been loaded and skips them on re-runs, making it safe to schedule repeatedly. It works in Databricks SQL, notebooks, and Jobs, and is the right choice when you expect files in the order of thousands over the table's lifetime — ad-hoc loads, one-time migrations, or pipelines where a small batch arrives daily. Auto Loader (the cloudFiles Structured Streaming source) is designed for millions of files or more: it uses cloud notification services or directory listing to detect new arrivals incrementally, requires fewer total listing API calls than COPY INTO at scale, and can split a large backlog across many micro-batches. Use Auto Loader for production streaming ingest and for any source with frequent, high-volume file drops. Auto Loader also offers richer schema inference and evolution primitives. Both tools support idempotent re-runs, but Auto Loader's Structured Streaming checkpoint is the more durable mechanism for long-running pipelines. The junior mistake is defaulting to COPY INTO in a loop when file counts will grow into the millions — listing cost becomes prohibitive.
fj: I'd use COPY INTO in a scheduled job for all my file ingestion — it handles duplicates automatically.
fs: COPY INTO is correct up to thousands of files lifetime but gets expensive at scale because it must scan the full source path. Auto Loader is the production choice for millions of files — it uses cloud event notifications or incremental listing to stay O(new files), not O(total files).

code.label: COPY INTO — idempotent batch load (thousands of files)
Auto Loader — streaming ingest (millions of files)
